name: HMS P 48 (P 48)
events: The history of HMS P 48 as compiled on this page is extracted from P 48's patrol reports and logbooks. Corrections and details regarding information from the enemy's side (for instance the composition of convoys attacked) is kindly provided by Mr. Platon Alexiades, a naval researcher from Canada.
This page was last updated in December 2018.
6 Jun 1942 At 1600 B/6, P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) is commissioned for trials at Barrow.
The submarine is not yet taken over from the shipyard. (1)
13 Jun 1942 P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) is docked at Barrow for a propeller change. (1)
16 Jun 1942 P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) is undocked at Barrow following which she was wiped (degaussed). (1)
17 Jun 1942 P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) conducted compass adjustment trials at Barrow.
At 1330 B/17, the submarine departed her builder's yard at Barrow for Holy Loch. She was escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN). (2)

18 Jun 1942 At 1400 B/18, P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) arrived at Holy Loch to begin a period of trials and training. On arrival at Holy Loch the submarine was taken over from the shipyard having successfully completed her acceptance trials. (2)
20 Jun 1942 HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) conducted independent exercises in Loch Long. (1)
21 Jun 1942 HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) conducted independent exercises in the Clyde area. (1)
22 Jun 1942 HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) conducted full power and deep diving trials in the Clyde area. (1)
24 Jun 1942 HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) conducted deep diving trials in the Clyde area following which she proceeded to the torpedo firing range at Arrochar. (1)
25 Jun 1942 HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (1)
26 Jun 1942 HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. Upon completion of these trials HMS P 48 returned to Holy Loch. (1)
30 Jun 1942 HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) conducted several trials in the Clyde area. These included gunnery, D/F calibration and MDU (Mine Detection Unit) trials.
[As no log for July 1942 is available some details for this month will be missing.] (1)
5 Jul 1942 HMS P 219 (Lt. N.L.A. Jewell, RN) and HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) conducted night attack exercises in the Clyde area with each other. (3)

15 Jul 1942 Around 0900 B/15, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) departed Holy Loch for Lerwick. She made part of the passage together with HMS P 511 (Lt. D.E.O. Watson, DSC, RN) which was en-route from Rothesay to Scapa Flow and joined around 1200 B/15.
The submarines were escorted by HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR). (4)
17 Jul 1942 HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) arrived at Lerwick from Holy Loch. (1)

18 Jul 1942 Around 1100 B/18, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) departed Lerwick for her 1st war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Norwegian Sea between 63°30'N and 64°20'N and 01°00'E and 05°00'E on an anti-Uboat patrol.
She is to proceed through positions 090° - Out Skerries - 5 miles and 63°21'N, 01°59'E en-route to the patrol area.
For the daily positions of HMS P 48 see the map below.
20 Jul 1942 At 0300 B/20, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) arrived in her patrol area. (6)

21 Jul 1942 At 1805 B/21, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) received the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 1726 B/21 which contained the following; Aircraft reported a U-Boat in position 62°12'N, 03°42'E at 1145 B/21. Enemy course was north at a speed of 4 knots. The enemy dived on being attacked.
This referred to an attack by a Hudson of 48 Squadron RAF (Aircraft 'Z') on U-405.
P 48 altered course to 080° to intercept.
At 2035 B/21, P 48 surfaced to try to intercept the enemy submarine. She dived again at 2216 B/21. The enemy submarine was not sighted. (6)

25 Jul 1942 At 1250 B/25, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) received the Deputy Director Intelligence Centre's (D.D.I.C.) signal timed 1144 B/25 to HNoMS Uredd (Lt. R.O. Rören) which contained the following; U-boat by direction finding at 0849 Z/25 [1049 B/25] between 62°00'N, 02°00'E and 62°30'N, 02°30'E, bound towards the Atlantic.
Lt. Faber set course on the surface at 10 knots to reach the south-west corner of P 48's patrol area.

The signal referred to U-176 which had was en-route from Kiel (via Kristiansand and Bergen for her 1st war patrol in the Atlantic. She had departed Bergen the previous afternoon. U-176 indeed had transmitted a signal around that time, at 1035 B/25 according to her KTB. (7)

26 Jul 1942 At 2045 B/26, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) received the Capt. S.9's signals timed 1731 B/26 which contained the following; Return to Lerwick so as to pass through position 63°29'N, 00°01'E at 0800 B/28 and then through position 60°06'N, 00°01'W to arrive at Lerwick at 1400 B/29. (6)

27 Jul 1942 At 0015 B/27, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) received the Flag Officer Submarines signal timed 2216 B/26 which contained the following; Aircraft report at 1740 B/26, a U-boat in position 61°59'N, 04°30'E. course 345°.
HMS P 48 altered course to intercept but sighted nothing.
This most likely must have referred to U-595 which was on passage from Kiel (via Kristiansand) into the Atlantic for her 1st war patrol.

U-595 reported seeing an aircraft at 1722 B/26 in German naval grid AN 2333 (which is centred in position 60°51'N, 04°00'E). [The position is quite a bit off but we have no better candidate. U-462 was also in the general area but reported nothing.] (8)
28 Jul 1942 At 0400 B/28, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) left patrol to return to Lerwick in compliance with the Capt. S.9's signal timed 1731 B/26. (6)
29 Jul 1942 At 1420 B/29, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) ended her 1st war patrol at Lerwick.

Around 2330 B/2, she departed for Holy Loch later with HMS P 219 (Lt. N.L.A. Jewell, RN), HMS P 614 (Lt. D.J. Beckley, RN) and HMS P 615 (Lt. P.E. Newstead, RN). They were escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN). (6)
31 Jul 1942 Around 1950 B/31, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) arrived at Holy Loch from Lerwick. (1)
1 Aug 1942 HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) conducted D/G and compass adjustment trials at Holy Loch. (9)
11 Aug 1942 Around 2015 B/11, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) departed Holy Loch for Rothesay Bay where she anchored around 2140 B/11. (10)
12 Aug 1942 Around 0300 B/12, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) departed Rothesay Bay for Gibraltar.
Passage through the Irish Sea was made together with HMS P 219 (Lt. N.L.A. Jewell, RN) and HrMs O 21 (Lt.Cdr. J.F. van Dulm, RNN).
The submarines were escorted by HrMs Jan van Gelder (Lt. P.L.M. van Geen, RNN) until around 2130 B/13 when they all parted company with each other in position 280° - Bishop Rock - 5 miles,.
From there HMS P 48 was routed as follows; Position 'E' = 49°44'N, 08°19'W. Position 'F' = 49°34'N, 12°25'W. Position 'D' = 43°01'N, 12°24'W, E.T.A. 1400 A/20.
Further routing would be signalled by the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.).

15 Aug 1942 At 1410 B/15 and 1530 B/15, in position 49°45'N, 11°06'W, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) sighted two and one fishing vessels respectively. Their names and numbers could not be read but all were flying the Dutch flag and were steam trawlers of about 200 tons. The trawls were seen to be out. (6)

16 Aug 1942 At 0700 B/16 and 0750 B/16, in position 49°21'N, 12°24'W, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) sighted two and one fishing vessels respectively. Their names and numbers could not be read but all were flying the French flag and were identified as tunny fishermen. The tunny rods were seen to be out.
The next day more of these vessels were sighted throughout the day. All flew the French flag and had their tunny rods rigged. (6)
17 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1340 A/17, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) and HMS P 219 (Lt. N.L.A. Jewell, RN) received the following orders from the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.)

Onward route on surface at the Commanding Officers discretion as follows 1) HMS P 48 through the following positions; 'G' = 37°55'N, 10°06'W at 0500 A/22. 'H' = 225° - Cape St. Vincent - 10 miles 1500 A/22. 'J' = 36°38'N, 07°15'W at 0100 A/23 to make rendezvous with the escort to Gibraltar in position 'K' = 000° - Cape Spartel - 5 miles at 1030 A/23.

2) P 219 through the following positions; 'L' = 37°22'N, 10°02'E at 0930 A/22. 'M' = 225° - Cape St. Vincent - 15 miles at 1500 A/22. 'N' = 36°22'N, 07°00'E at 0130 A/23 to make rendezvous with the escort to Gibraltar in position 'K' = 000° - Cape Spartel - 5 miles at 0800 A/23.

3) All times zone minus one. 4) Total bombing restrictions are arranged. 5) Both submarines will be escorted Gibraltar from position 'K'. 6) If unable to comply within two hours report position course and speed. 7) P 48 and P 219 may encounter one another on 22nd and 23rd. Submarines are not to be attacked on these days unless definitely identified as hostile. (12)
19 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 0406 A/19 the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) and HMS P 219 (Lt. N.L.A. Jewell, RN);

Proceed with despatch to patrol in the following positions; P 48, 44°01'N, 12°25'W. P 219, 43°02'N, 11°01'W. Passing through position 43°02'N, 13°30'W thence due East to your patrol positions. Total bombing restrictions have been arranged.
The passage to Gibraltar now thus became the second war patrol for both these submarines. (12)

20 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1257 A/20, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN); Proceed with despatch to position 44°38'N, 12°00'W. Aircraft report position, course and speed of a 8000 ton merchant ship with funnel aft as 43°31'N, 09°19'W, 300°, 8 knots at 0303 A/20. Total bombing restrictions arranged.

This most likely referred to the German tanker Uckermark (former Altmark, 10698 GRT, built 1938) which was on passage from Western France to Japan. Due her repeatedly being sighted by aircraft the tanker broke off her attempt to break out and she arrived back at Le Verdon around 2015 B/20.
At 1315 A/20, the auxiliary machinery space reported flooding so surfaced.
At 1415 A/20, course was set to 163 at 9 knots making for Vigo, Spain.

At 1430 A/20, P 48 passed her signal timed 1338 A/20 to the Flag Officer Submarines, which contained the following; Auxiliary machine space flooded, water entering through ventilating trunk. Unable to dive. Am making for Vigo. Request immediate assistance. My speed of advance is 9 knots.

At 1549 A/20, HMS P 48 passed her signal timed 1455 A/20 to the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) which contained the following; My 1338 A/20. Auxiliary Machinery space inlet flood through log tube. Water ceased to enter. Unable to dive. Proceeding to Gibraltar through positions 'G', 'H' and 'K'. Assistance not now required. My position 44°01'N, 12° 15'E, speed 9 knots. Shifting to North Atlantic W/T organization at 2200 A/20th.

At 1628 A/20, the F.O.S.'s signal timed 1552 A/20 was received which contained the following; Your 1338 A/20. Immediate assistance impossible. What tank is damaged. Can you not shut off or block ventilating trunk. Report position, course and speed. Prefix reply immediate.

At 2035 A/20, the F.O.C.N.A.'s signal timed 1736 A/20 was received which contained the following; Your 1455 A/20. 1) Trawler HMS Alouette (T/Lt. R. Adams, RNR) is proceeding from vicinity of Lisbon to rendezvous with you at daylight/21 and escort you to Gibraltar. 2). Alouette has been informed of your movements. 3) Total bombing restrictions are arranged. (13)

21 Aug 1942 At 2150 A/21, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) received the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic's (F.O.C.N.A.) signal timed 1906 A/21 which contained the following; HMS Alouette (T/Lt. R. Adams, RNR) in position 37°45'N, 09°55'W at 1800 B/21 has been informed your estimated position at 2000 B/21 was 39°57'N, 10°47'W proceeding through position 'G' and 'H' at 9 knots, has now been ordered to rendezvous with you in daylight/22 and to report if not met by 1200 B/22. (11)
22 Aug 1942 At 0840 B/22, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) sighted a trawler bearing 095° which was soon identified as HMS Alouette (T/Lt. R. Adams, RNR).
Twenty minutes later they joined company and proceeded to Gibraltar. (6)
23 Aug 1942 At 1710 B/23, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) ended her 2nd war patrol at Gibraltar. (6)
25 Aug 1942 HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) is docked in No. 1 graving dock at Gibraltar for repairs. (10)
20 Sep 1942 HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) is undocked at Gibraltar. (14)
24 Sep 1942 HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) conducted exercises off Gibraltar. (14)
25 Sep 1942 HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) conducted exercises off Gibraltar together with HMS Jonquil (Lt.Cdr. R.E.H. Partington, RD, RNR) and HMS Coltsfoot (Lt.Cdr. the Hon. W.K. Rous, RNVR). (14)
26 Sep 1942 HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) conducted exercises off Gibraltar together with HMS Parthian (Lt.Cdr. D. St. Clair-Ford, RN). (14)
27 Sep 1942 HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) conducted exercises off Gibraltar. (14)

29 Sep 1942 At 1600 A/29, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) departed Gibraltar for her 3rd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Alboran Sea north of 36°20'N and between 03°37'W and 04°10'W.
[As no log (for October) or patrol report is available no map can be displayed.]

In a signal timed 1736 A/29, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following orders to HMS P 48; HMS Clyde (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN) carried out trimming dive about 4 miles east Of Gibraltar. Enemy aircraft still in Vicinity. Proceed diving by day. (14)
6 Oct 1942 Around 0930 A/6, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) ended her 3rd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean) at Gibraltar. (15)
This convoy was assembled off Oversay on 23 October 1942.

It was made up of the following transports; Alcinous (Dutch, 6189 GRT, built 1925), Alphard (Dutch, 5483 GRT, built 1937), Ardeola (British, 2609 GRT, built 1912), Benalbanach (British, 7153 GRT, built 1940), Charles H. Cramp (American, 6220 GRT, built 1920), Chattanooga City (American, 5687 GRT, built 1921), City of Worcester (British, 5469 GRT, built 1927), Clan MacTaggart (British, 7622 GRT, built 1920), Delilian (British, 6423 GRT, built 1923), Edward Ruthledge (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), Empire Confidence (British, 5023 GRT, built 1925), Empire Mordred (British, 7024 GRT, built 1942), Fort McLoughlin (British, 7129 GRT, built 1942), Glenfinlas (British, 7479 GRT, built 1917), Havildar (British, 5401 GRT, built 1940), Hopecrown (British, 5180 GRT, built 1937), Jean Jadot (Belgian, 5859 GRT, built 1929), Lalande (British, 7453 GRT, built 1920), Lochmonar (British, 9412 GRT, built 1924), Lycaon (British, 7350 GRT, built 1913), Macharda (British, 7998 GRT, built 1938), Manchester Port (British, 7071 GRT, built 1935), Mark Twain (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Maron (British, 6487 GRT, built 1930), Mary Slessor (British, 5027 GRT, built 1930), Ocean Rider (British, 7178 GRT, built 1942), Ocean Viceroy (British, 7174 GRT, built 1942), Ocean Volga (British, 7174 GRT, built 1942), Ocean Wanderer (British, 7178 GRT, built 1942), Pacific Exporter (British, 6734 GRT, built 1928), Recorder (British, 5981 GRT, built 1930), Salacia (British, 5495 GRT, built 1937), Sobo (British, 5353 GRT, built 1937), St. Essylt (British, 5634 GRT, built 1941), Stanhill (British, 5969 GRT, built 1942), Tadorna (British, 1947 GRT, built 1928), Theseus (British, 6527 GRT, built 1908), Tiba (Dutch, 5239 GRT, built 1938), Urlana (British, 6852 GRT, built 1941), Walt Whitman (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), William M. Floyd (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), William Wirt (American, 7191 GRT, built 1942) and Zebulon B. Vance (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942).
Also part of the convoy were the landing ships Derwentdale (8390 GRT, built 1941), Dewdale (8265 GRT, built 1941) and Ennerdale (8280 GRT, built 1941).

On assembly the convoy was escorted by the escort carrier HMS Avenger (Cdr. A.P. Colthurst, RN), AA ship HMS Alynbank (A/Capt.(Retd.) H.F. Nash, RN), destroyer HMS Vansittart (Lt.Cdr. T. Johnston, RN), sloops HMS Deptford (Lt.Cdr. H.R. White, RN), HMS Stork (Cdr. G.N. Brewer, RN), corvettes HMS Convolvulus (A/Lt.Cdr. R.F.R. Yarde-Buller, RNVR), HMS Gardenia (T/Lt. M.M. Firth, RNVR), HMS Marigold (Lt. J.A.S. Halcrow, RD, RNR), HMS Pentstemon (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) J. Byron, DSC, RNR), HMS Rhododendron (Lt.Cdr. L.A. Sayers, RNR), HMS Samphire (Lt.Cdr. F.T. Renny, DSC, RNR), HMS Vetch (T/A/Lt.Cdr. H.J. Beverley, DSO, DSC, RNR), HMS Violet (Lt. C.N. Stewart, RNR) and the minesweepers HMS Acute (Lt.Cdr. D. Lampen, DSO, RN), HMS Alarm (T/Lt.Cdr. R. Patterson, SANF(V)), HMS Albacore (Lt.Cdr. J.D.L. Williams, RN) and HMS Cadmus (Lt.Cdr. J.B.G. Temple, DSC, RN).

Around 1000 A/4, the convoy was split up into two sections KMS A1 and KMS O1. KMS A1 was destined for Algiers and KMS O1 was destined for Oran. KMS O1 then proceeded to the westwards so as to pass the Straits of Gibraltar later.

Convoy KMS A 1 was to pass the Strait of Gibraltar around 2345 A/5; it was made up of the transports; City of Worcester, Glenfinlas, Jean Jadot, Lalande, Lochmonar, Macharda, Manchester Port, Maron, Ocean Rider, Ocean Viceroy, Ocean Volga, Ocean Wanderer, Sobo, Stanhill, Tiba and Urlana.
The landing ships Dewdale and Ennerdale were also part of the convoy.

The convoy was escorted by the sloop HMS Stork, corvettes HMS Convolvulus, HMS Marigold, HMS Pentstemon, HMS Samphire [this corvette might have already parted company though, see below] and the minesweepers HMS Acute, HMS Alarm, HMS Albacore and HMS Cadmus.
Around 0700 A/5, the corvette HMS Samphire arrived at Gibraltar with defects from convoy KMS A1.

Around 0800 A/5, the minesweepers HMS Algerine (Lt.Cdr. W.A. Cooke, RN), HMS Hussar (Lt. R.C. Biggs, DSO, DSC, RN) and HMS Speedwell (Lt.Cdr. T.E. Williams, RNR) departed Gibraltar to join convoy KMS A1.

Around 1830 A/5, the M/S trawlers HMS Cava (T/Lt. R.L. Petty-Major, RNVR), HMS Juliet (Lt. L.B. Moffatt, RNR), HMS Othello (T/Lt. S.C. Dickinson, RNVR), HMS Stroma (Skr. J.S. Harper, RNR), HMS Hoy (T/Lt. G.H. McNair, MBE, RNVR), HMS Inchcolm (Skr. A.C. Whitcombe, RNR), HMS Mull (Lt. J. Plomer, RCNVR), HMS Rysa (T/Lt. J.H. Cooper, RNVR) and the motor launches ML 238, ML 273, ML 283, ML 295, ML 307, ML 336, ML 338, ML 444 departed Gibraltar to join convoy KMS A1.

Around 2230 A/5, the monitor HMS Roberts (Capt. J.G.Y. Loveband, RN), escort destroyers HMS Bicester (Lt.Cdr. S.W.F. Bennetts, RN), HMS Bramham (Lt. E.F. Baines, DSO, RN), HMS Cowdray (Lt.Cdr. C.W. North, RN), HMS Zetland (Lt. J.V. Wilkinson, RN) and the corvette HMS Samphire (with her repairs completed) departed Gibraltar to join convoy KMS A1.

Convoy KMS O 1 was to pass the Strait of Gibraltar around 1630 A/6; it was made up of the transports; Alcinous, Alphard, Benalbanach, Charles H. Cramp, Chattanooga City, Clan Mactaggart, Delinlian, Edward Rutledge, Empire Confidence, Empire Mordred, Havildar, Lycaon, Mark Twain, Mary Slessor, Pacific Exporter, Recorder, Salacia, St. Essylt, Thesues, Walt Whitman, William Floyd, William Wirt and Zebulon B. Vance.
The landing ship Derwentdale was also part of this convoy.
The convoy was escorted by the AA ship HMS Alynbank, sloop HMS Deptford, corvettes HMS Gardenia, HMS Rhododendron, HMS Vetch and HMS Violet.

Around 1500 A/6, the minesweepers HMS Brixham (Lt. G.A. Simmers, RNR), HMS Bude (Lt. F.A.J. Andrew, RN), HMS Clacton (A/Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) L.S. Shaw, RNR) and HMS Felixstowe (T/Lt. C.G. Powney, RNVR) departed Gibraltar to join the convoy KMS O1.

After dark on the 6th, the M/S trawlers HMS Coriolanus (T/Lt. N. Hunt, RNVR), HMS Eday (T/Lt. W.Y. Surtees, RNR), HMS Inchmarnock (T/Lt. C.G.V. Corneby, RNR), HMS Kerrera (Skr. R.W. Slater, RNR) and the motor launches ML 280, ML 458, ML 463, ML 469, ML 471, ML 480, ML 483 and HDML 1127, HDML 1128 and HDML 1139 departed Gibraltar to join convoy KMS O1.

Two ships from Convoy KMS 1, the Ardeola and Tadorna formed part of Convoy KMS 1A after the convoy had split up. They were to proceed to Malta unescorted. The Admiralty had decided to make use of the expected confusion of the landings in North Africa to run two 'small' merchant ships with important cargo to Malta. These ships were considered expendable. They parted company with convoy KMS 1A on 8 November. They did not reach Malta however. When off Cape Bon on 9 November, they were taken under fire by Vichy French coastal batteries, despite the darkness, and then captured by motor torpedo boats. They were brought into Bizerta where their cargo was unloaded. The ships were later taken over by the Italians.
This convoy was assembled off Oversay on 26 October 1942.

It was made up of the following (troop) transports; Awatea (British, 13482 GRT, built 1936), Batory (Polish, 14287 GRT, built 1936), Cathay (British, 15225 GRT, built 1925), Dempo (Dutch, 17024 GRT, built 1931), Derbyshire (British, 11660 GRT, built 1935), Duchess of Bedford (British, 20123 GRT, built 1928), Durban Castle (British, 17388 GRT, built 1938), Ettrick (British, 11279 GRT, built 1938), Exceller (American, 6597 GRT, built 1941), Leinster (British, 4302 GRT, built 1937) Letitia (British, 13595 GRT, built 1925), Llangibby Castle (British, 11951 GRT, built 1929), Marnix van St. Aldegonde (Dutch, 19355 GRT, built 1930), Monarch of Bermuda (British, 22424 GRT, built 1931), Mooltan (British, 20952 GRT, built 1923), Nieuw Zeeland (Dutch, 11069 GRT, built 1928), Orbita (British, 15495 GRT, built 1915), Otranto (British, 20026 GRT, built 1925), Reina del Pacifico (British, 17702 GRT, built 1931), Sobieski (Polish, 11030 GRT, built 1939), Strathnaver (British, 22283 GRT, built 1931), Tegelberg (Dutch, 14150 GRT, built 1937), Viceroy of India (British, 19627 GRT, built 1929), Warwick Castle (British, 20107 GRT, built 1930) and Winchester Castle (British, 20012 GRT, built 1930).

The headquarters ships HMS Bulolo (Capt.(Retd.) R.L. Hamer, RN), HMS Largs (Cdr. E.A. Divers, OBE, RNR), the landing ships HMS Glengyle (Capt.(Retd.) D.S. McGrath, RN), HMS Karanja (Lt.Cdr.(Emgy.) D.S. Hore-Lacy, RN), HMS Keren (A/Cdr. S.E. Crewe-Read, RN), HMS Princess Beatrix (Cdr.(Retd.) T.B. Brunton, DSC, RN), HMS Queen Emma (Capt.(Retd.) G.L.D. Gibbs, DSO and Bar, RN), HMS Royal Scotsman (Lt.Cdr. J.D. Armstrong, DSC, RD, RNR), HMS Royal Ulsterman (A/Lt.Cdr. W.R.K. Clark, DSC, RD RNR) and HMS Ulster Monarch (Lt.Cdr. N.A.F. Kingscote, RNR) and the attack transports USS Almaack (T/Capt. C.L. Nichols, USN), USS Leedstown (Cdr. D. Cook, USNR), USS Samuel Chase (Capt. R.C. Heimer, USCG) and USS Thomas Stone (Capt. O.R. Bennehoff, USN) were also part of the convoy.

On assembly off Oversay on the 27th the convoy was escorted by the light cruiser HMS Sheffield (Capt. A.W. Clarke, RN, flying the flag of Rear-Admiral C.H.J. Harcourt, CBE, RN), escort carrier HMS Biter (Capt. E.M.C. Abel Smith, RN), destroyer HMS Clare (Lt.Cdr. L.H. Landman, RN), sloops HMS Aberdeen (Lt.Cdr. H. Day, RN), HMS Enchantress (Lt.Cdr. A.E.T. Christie, OBE, RN), HMS Ibis (Lt.Cdr. H.M. Darell-Brown, RN), cutters HMS Hartland (Lt.Cdr. G.P. Billot, RNR), HMS Walney (Lt.Cdr. P.C. Meyrick, RN), frigates HMS Exe (A/Cdr. M.A.O. Biddulph, DSC, RN), HMS Rother (Lt.Cdr. R.V.E. Case, DSC and Bar, RD, RNR), HMS Spey (Cdr. H.G. Boys-Smith, DSO and Bar, RD, RNR), HMS Swale (Lt.Cdr. J. Jackson, RNR) and HMS Tay (Lt.Cdr. R.E. Sherwood, RNR).
Around 1120 A/2, the destroyers HrMs Isaac Sweers (Capt. W. Harmsen, RNN) and HMS Escapade (Cdr. E.N.V. Currey, DSC, RN) joined coming from the Azores.

Around 0200 A/3, the AA ships HMS Palomares (A/Capt.(Retd.) J.H. Jauncey, RN), HMS Pozarica (Capt.(Retd.) L.B. Hill, DSO, OBE, RN) and the destroyers HMS Achates (Lt.Cdr. A.H.T. Johns, RN), HMS Antelope (Lt.Cdr. E.N. Sinclair, RN), HMS Amazon (Lt.Cdr.(Emgy.) Lord Teynham, RN), HMS Velox (Lt. G.B. Barstow, RN), HMS Verity, (Lt.Cdr. R. Horncastle, RN), HMS Westcott (Cdr. I.H. Bockett-Pugh, DSO, RN) and HMS Wishart (Cdr. H.G. Scott, RN) departed Gibraltar to join the convoy. At 1045 A/3, the destroyer HMS Wivern (Cdr. M.D.C. Meyrick, RN) also departed to join the convoy. She had been unable to depart earlier due to defects.
Around 0800 A/3, the destroyer HMS Marne (Lt.Cdr. H.N.A. Richardson, DSO, DSC, RN) joined the convoy coming from the Azores.
Around 1300 A/3, the light cruiser HMS Jamaica (Capt. J.L. Storey, RN) also departed Gibraltar to join the convoy.
Around 1830 Z/3, HMS Sheffield parted company with the convoy to proceed to Gibraltar where she arrived at 0815 A/3, she was to fuel and then join ' Force O '.

Around noon on 4 November 1942, the convoy was split up into two sections KMF A1 and KMF O1. KMF A1 was destined for Algiers and KMF O1 was destined for Oran. KMF O1 then proceeded to the westwards so as to pass the Straits of Gibraltar later.

Convoy KMF A 1 was to pass the Strait of Gibraltar around 0100 A/6; it was made up of the (troop) transports; Almaack, Awatea, Cathay, Dempo, Ettrick, Exceller, Leedstown, Marnix van St. Aldegonde, Otranto, Sobieski, Strathnaver, Viceroy of India and Winchester Castle.

The headquarters ship HMS Bulolo and the landing ships HMS Karanja, HMS Keren, HMS Royal Scotsman, HMS Royal Ulsterman and Ulster Monarch and the attack transports USS Samuel Chase and USS Thomas Stone were also part of the convoy.
[exactly which ships of the escort went on with this part of the convoy will have to be researched further.]

In the morning of 5 November, HrMs Isaac Sweers parted company with the convoy to join ' Force H '. HMS Escapade and HMS Marne were apparently detached to Gibraltar on the convoy passing the Strait of Gibraltar.
Also on 5 November, the corvettes HMS Spiraea (Lt.Cdr. R.S. Miller, DSC, RNR) and HMS Jonquil (Lt.Cdr. R.E.H. Partington, RD, RNR) departed Gibraltar to join convoy KMF A1.

Around 0200 A/6, the destroyers HMS Broke (Lt.Cdr. A.F.C. Layard, RN), HMS Malcolm (A/Cdr. A.B. Russell, RN), HMS Vanoc ( A/Cdr. C.F.H. Churchill, RN) and HMS Wrestler (Lt. R.W.B. Lacon, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar to join convoy KMF A1 and relieve HMS Achates, HMS Antelope, HMS Amazon and HMS Wivern. After having been relieved these destroyers arrived at Gibraltar around 0545 A/6. Also arriving at Gibraltar were the Leinster, HMS Royal Scotsman, HMS Royal Ulsterman and Ulster Monarch.

Around 1000 A/6, HMS Broke, HMS Malcolm, HMS Vanoc and HMS Wrestler joined ' Force O ' while the screen on ' Force O ' joined the convoy, the destroyers / escort destroyers involved were ORP Blyskawica (Lt.Cdr. L. Lichodziejewski, ORP), HMS Lamerton (Lt.Cdr. C.R. Purse, DSC, RN), HMS Wheatland (Lt.Cdr. R. de L. Brooke, DSC, RN) and HMS Wilton (Lt. A.P. Northey, DSC, RN). The AA ship HMS Tynwald (Capt.(Retd.) P.G. Wodehouse, DSO, RN) also joined the convoy from ' Force O ' at the same time.

Around 0535 A/7, in position 37°34'N, 00°01'W, the attack transport USS Thomas Stone was torpedoed and damaged by an enemy aircraft. HMS Spey remained with the damaged ship. At 2040 A/7, the destroyers HMS Wishart and HMS Velox joined and the ship was taken in tow by HMS Wishart. HMS Spey by that time had departed with the ships 24 landing craft in which the ships troops had embarked. She was to escort them to Algiers but all had to be scuttled and the troops were taken on board HMS Spey. At 0535 A/8 the tug St. Day joined which also passed a tow. The damaged ship anchored off Algiers around 1030 A/11 being towed there by HMS Wishart and HMS St. Day.
Around 0725 Z/7, HMS Clare parted company to join ' Force O ' which she did around 0913 Z/7.

Around 1815 A/7, the section destined for ' C Sector ' (Charlie Sector) parted company with the convoy. It was made up of the USS Almaack, USS Leedstown, USS Samuel Chase, Exceller and Dempo. With them were also transports from convoy KMS A1. They were escorted by the AA ship HMS Tynwald, escort destroyers HMS Cowdray, HMS Zetland, sloop HMS Enchantress, minesweepers HMS Algerine, HMS Hussar, HMS Speedwell, corvettes HMS Pentstemon, HMS Samphire, MS trawlers HMS Cava, HMS Othello and the motor launches HMS ML 273 and HMS ML 295. At 2135 A/7, the beacon submarine HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) made contact with the force and the ships were guided to their positions for the landings. From convoy KMS A1 the transports Macharda and Maron were destined for Charlie sector. They were escorted by the sloop HMS Stork and the corvettes HMS Pentstemon and HMS Samphire.
Around 1900 A/7, The remainder of convoy KMF A1 split into two sections, one for ' A Sector ' (Apple Sector) and one for ' B Sector ' (Beer Sector).

The force for ' A Sector ' was made up of HMS Karanja and the Marnix van St. Aldegonde and Viceroy of India. With them were also transports from convoy KMS A1. They were escorted by the AA ship HMS Pozarica, escort destroyers HMS Bicester, HMS Bramham, frigate HMS Rother, minesweeper HMS Cadmus, MS trawlers HMS Juliet, HMS Rysa, HMS Stroma and the motor launches HMS ML 283, HMS ML 336 and HMS ML 338. At 2214 A/7, the made contact with their beacon submarine HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN). A few minutes later they stopped and the landings commenced. From convoy KMS A1 the following ships were assigned to ' A Sector '; Dewdale, Lalande, Manchester Port, Ocean Viceroy and Ocean Wanderer. They were escorted by the corvettes HMS Convolvulus and HMS Marigold.

The force for ' B Sector ' was made up of HMS Bulolo, HMS Keren and the Awatea, Cathay, Otranto, Sobieski, Strathnaver and Winchester Castle. With them were also transports from convoy KMS A1. They were escorted by the AA ship HMS Palomeres, destroyer ORP Blyskawica, escort destroyers HMS Lamerton, HMS Wheatland, HMS Wilton, minesweepers HMS Acute, HMS Alarm, HMS Albacore, MS trawlers HMS Hoy, HMS Incholm, HMS Mull and the motor launches HMS ML 238, HMS ML 307 and HMS ML 444. They made contact with their beacon submarine HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) around 2220 A/7 hours and landing operation commenced shortly afterwards. From convoy KMS A1 the following ships were assigned to ' A Sector '; City of Worcester, Ennerdale, Glenfinlas, Jean Jadot, Lochmonar, Ocean Rider, Ocean Volga, Sobo, Stanhill, Tiba and Urlana. They were escorted by the sloop HMS Stork and the corvettes HMS Pentstemon and HMS Samphire which then went on with the ships for the ' Charlie sector '.
On 9 November the ships involved in the landings anchored in Algiers Bay.

Convoy KMF O 1 was to pass the Strait of Gibraltar around 2230 A/6; it was made up of the (troop) transports; Batory, Duchess of Bedford, Durban Castle, Letitia, Llangibby Castle, Monarch of Bermuda, Mooltan, Nieuw Zeeland, Orbita, Reina del Pacifico, Tegelberg and Warwick Castle.
The headquarters ship HMS Largs and the landing ships HMS Glengyle, HMS Princess Beatrix and HMS Queen Emma were also part of the convoy.
Around 1950 A/4, the light cruiser HMS Aurora (Capt. W.G. Agnew, CB, RN) departed Gibraltar to join convoy KMF O1.
For the landings at Oran three main beaches were selected. ' X ', ' Y ' and ' Z ' beach. There was also one subsidiary beach, ' R '.
The fast convoy, KMF O1, would, after passing through the Straits of Gibraltar make rendezvous with the slow convoy, KMS O1 in position 36°26'N, 01°15'W.

The convoys would then be diverted into nine groups, these were; For ' X ' beach Group I, 1st Division; Batory, HMS Princess Beatrix, Queen Emma, 2nd Division; Benalbenach, Mark Twain, Mary Slessor and Walt Whitman. They were escorted by the light cruiser HMS Aurora, destroyer HMS Wivern, corvettes HMS Gardenia, HMS Vetch and the motor launch HMS HDML 1139. Group VIII, LST HMS Bachaquero (A/Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) A.W. McMullan, RNR) escorted by the M/S trawler HMS Horatio (T/Lt. C.A. Lemkey, RNR).

For ' Y ' beach Group II; HMS Glengyle, Monarch of Bermuda, Llangibby Castle, Clan Mactaggart and Salacia. They were escorted by the destroyers Brilliant, HMS Verity, M/S trawlers HMS Coriolanus, HMS Eday, HMS Inchmarnock, HMS Kerrera and the motor launches HMS ML 458, HMS ML 463, HMS ML 469, HMS ML 471 and HMS HDML 1128.

For ' Z ' beach Group III, 1st Division; Duchess of Bedford, Durban Castle, Ettrick, Warwick Castle. 2nd Division; Derwentdale, Reina del Pacifico and Tegelberg. They were escorted by the light cruiser HMS Jamaica, escort destroyers HMS Calpe (Lt.Cdr. H. Kirkwood, DSC, RN), HMS Farndale (Cdr. D.P. Trentham, RN), minesweepers HMS Brixham, HMS Bude, HMS Clacton, HMS Felixtowe, HMS Polruan (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) J.S. Landers, RNR), HMS Rothesay (Cdr. A.A. Martin, DSC, RD, RNR), HMS Rhyl (Cdr. L.J.S. Ede, DSO, RN), HMS Stornoway (T/A/Lt.Cdr. C.R. Fraser, RNR) and the motor launches HMS ML 280, HMS HDML 1127.

Group V; Alcinous, Alphard, Charles H. Cramp, Chatanooga City, Delilian, Recorder and Zebulon B. Vance. They were escorted by the sloop HMS Deptford, cutters HMS Hartland, HMS Walney, corvettes HMS Rhododendron, HMS Violet and the motor launches HMS ML 480 and HMS ML 483.
Group VI, 1st division; Derbyshire, Letitia, Mooltan and Nieuw Zeeland. 2nd division, Empire Confidence, Lycaon and Theseus.

Group VII, 1st division, Empire Mordred, Havildar, Pacific Exporter and St. Essylt. 2nd division; Edward Rutledge, William Floyd and William Wirt. Groups VI and VII were escorted by the light (AA) cruiser HMS Delhi (Capt. A.T.G.C. Peachey, RN), destroyer HMS Vansittart, sloop HMS Aberdeen and the frigates HMS Exe and HMS Swale.

Group IX; LST's HMS Misoa (T/Lt. K.G. Graham, RNR) and HMS Tasajera (Lt.Cdr. W.E. Gelling, DSC, RD, RNR). They were escorted by the M/S trawlers HMS Fluellen (T/Lt. B.J. Hampson, RNR), HMS Ronaldsay (T/Lt. A. Stirling, RNR) and HMS Shiant (T/Lt. A.C. Elton, RNR).
For ' R ' beach Group IV; HMS Royal Scotsman, HMS Royal Ulsterman and HMS Ulster Monarch. They had the same escort as Group III.
Two submarines were stationed off the beaches as beacons, these were HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) and HMS P 54 (Lt. C.E. Oxborrow, DSC, RN).

31 Oct 1942 Around 0700 A/31, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) departed Gibraltar for her 4th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to perform a special operation (during operation Torch, landing beach marking off Algiers). She carried only six torpedoes to make room for two Folbots. She was to proceed to Malta at the end of this patrol to join the 10th Submarine Flotilla based there.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 48 see the map below.

1 Nov 1942 At 0030 Z/1, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) sighted a merchant vessel which was burning it's lights bearing 140°. Course of this vessel was 300°. [No position is given in the patrol report and no log is available for this month.]
At 1818 Z/1, HE was picked up on bearing 085°. It was very clear and sounded like diesel.
At 1824 Z/1, the HE grew weaker finally fading out on the same bearing of 085° four minutes later.
At 2142 Z/1, HMS P 48 received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1506 A/1, in which it was stated that Operation Torch would go ahead and that 'D'-day was 8 November.
Between 2215 Z/1 and 2305 Z/1, Folbot exercises were carried out. (6)
2 Nov 1942 At 1954 Z/2, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 0940 A/2, in which it was she was ordered to carry out preliminary reconnaissance by Folbot.
Between 2150 Z/2 and 2240 Z/2 Folbot exercises were carried out. (6)
3 Nov 1942 At 1337 Z/3, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) arrived in her assigned area off Algiers when she sighted the Cape Caxing Lighthouse bearing 119° at a range of 17.5 miles.
At 1845 Z/3, HMS P 48 surfaced.
At 2115 Z/3, HMS P 48 commenced to close the beaches of 'B' (Beer) sector for a night reconnaissance. Only two fishing vessels were seen.
At 2320 Z/3, HMS P 48 was in position 079° - Cape Caxine - 5.6 miles.
At 2340 Z/3, the reconnaissance was completed and HMS P 48 proceeded to seaward. (6)
4 Nov 1942 At 0506 Z/4, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN), dived.
At 0615 Z/4, a merchant vessel was sighted bearing 250°.

At 0645 Z/4, the merchant vessel passed at a range of 1900 yards on course 085° at a speed of 8 knots. The vessel was in ballast and was flying the French flag which was also painted on her sides. The name could be read, it was the Moron (4128 GRT, built 1930).
At 0930 Z/4, HMS P 48 proceeded inshore to carry out a periscope reconnaissance.
At 1500 Z/4, HMS P 48 was in the position from which she was to carry out her beacon duties.
At 1530 Z/4, the periscope reconnaissance was completed. Two trawlers were in the area during the afternoon but they did not approach very close.
At 1845 Z/4, HMS P 48 surfaced.
At 2115 Z/4, a Folbot was launched for a night reconnaissance.
At 0035 Z/4, the Folbot was re-embarked. (6)

6 Nov 1942 At 1025 Z/6, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) sighted a merchant vessel bearing 150° at a range of 7 miles. Course of the merchant vessel was 255°. The vessel was estimated to be around 2000 tons.
At 1512 Z/6, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 0950 A/6 was received in which the position of beach 'B' (Beer) White and the Folbot marking positions were confirmed. (6)

7 Nov 1942 During the night of 7/8 November 1942, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) assisted in the landing at Algiers Bay. She launched the folbot and her party and also guided the convoy in, acting as beacon.
1830 Z/7 - Surfaced and charged. Also got a Folbot on the casing.
1930 Z/7 - Proceeded inshore.
2035 Z/7 - Released the Folbot in position 261° - Cape Caxine lighthouse - 4.8 miles. P 48 then proceeded to the convoy release position. Up to now all shore lights were burning normally.
2120 Z/7 - HMS P 48 arrived in the convoy release position.
2125 Z/7 - The Capt. S.8's signal timed 1342 A/7 was received which contained orders for P 48's movements on completion of the operations. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.]
2130 Z/7 - Commenced to transmit to home the convoy in.
2145 Z/7 - Sighted the convoy bearing 290° at a range of 5 miles.
2215 Z/7 - The convoy arrived in the release position.

2325 Z/7 - Waited in vain for the motor launch to come and take off the pilot so summoned a small landing craft alongside and disembarked the pilot then proceeded with all despatch to the beacon position.
0001 Z/8 - Arrived in the beacon position and commenced to flash a white light in accordance with the orders.
0050 Z/8 - The first flight passed.
0250 Z/8 - Proceeded to the release position. There was no sign of the convoy or trawler escort which should have had HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN) in company.
0601 Z/7 - In touch with the convoy. Requested an escort to take the submarine to the submarine sanctuary.
0650 Z/7 - Proceeded to the submarine sanctuary escorted by HMS Albacore (Lt.Cdr. J.D.L. Williams, RN).
1130 Z/7 - Parted company with HMS Albacore. Dived.
2100 Z/7 - Surfaced and proceeded to the east in accordance with the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1342 A/7. (6)

9 Nov 1942 At 0846 Z/9, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0821 A/9 which ordered her to proceed through positions 38°16'N, 07°56'E and 38°30'N, 10°00'E. Further orders would follow. (16)
10 Nov 1942 At 2124 A/10, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1145 A/10 which ordered her to patrol off Cape San Vito as part of a patrol line. (6)
10 Nov 1942 At 2124 A/10, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1145 A/10 which ordered her to patrol off Cape San Vito as part of a patrol line.

A patrol line, laid out off Cape San Vito in positions 'F', 'G', 'H', 'J' and 'K' is to be relieved. The patrol line was currently occupied by the following submarines, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) was in position 'F' (315° - Cape San Vito - 3 miles), the other positions were on the same bearing 10 miles between them. Position 'G' was occupied by HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN), 'H' by HMS P 212 (Lt. J.H. Bromage, DSC, RN), 'J' by HMS P 247 (Lt. M.G.R. Lumby, DSC, RN). Position 'K' was vacant.
HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN), HMS P 48 approaching from the west were to take up position 'K', 'G' and 'H' respectively. Further orders would follow. (6)

11 Nov 1942 At 0042 Z/11, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2245 A/10 which contained the following orders; Your onward route, through positions; 'A' = 39°11'N, 12°38'E. waiting position 39°24'N, 12°26'E until 0530 A/13th. Position 'B' = 38°48'N, 13°10'E for landfall off Ustica. Position 'C' = 38°52'N, 12°55'E. Thence to arrive at position 'H' = 38°27'N, 12°23'E not before 0400 A/14.

At 0409 A/11, HMS P 48 received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2233 A/10 which contained the following orders; Following orders apply to submarines operating east of 08°00'E. 1) No submarine is to be attacked unless its hostile identity is established beyond reasonable doubt. 2) Submarines are not mutually to establish identity by flashing recognition signals. If in pursuit of enemy and another submarine is sighted which is not definitely identified as enemy it is to be watched and course adjusted to draw clear. If not in pursuit of enemy the submarine making the sighting is to dive and use super sonic telegraphy (S.S./T.) to endeavour to establish identity. 3) Italy bound merchant ships are not to be attacked. Salvoes are limited to four torpedoes except during an attack on battleships. 4) Submarines on patrol lines should communicate and exchange positions by S.S./T. once daily.
At 1950 A/11, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1754 A/11 was received. Course was altered to comply as P 48 was now to patrol off Bizerta. (16)

12 Nov 1942 At 1219 A/12, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1034 A/12 which contained the following information; Enemy report time of origin (T.O.O.), 0718 A/12. These two ships will be reconnaissanced at noon, since Tunis is blocked consider destination probably Bizerta.
The aircraft sighting report with the T.O.O. 0718 A/12 contained the following; Position of two merchant vessels and five destroyers 38°00'N, 12°02'E, course 100°, speed 10 knots.
[Citta di Napoli-convoy from Naples to Bizerta ???]

At 1428 A/12, the Officer of the Watch reported the top of a funnel and the masts of two merchant vessels escorted by at least one destroyer and four aircraft, bearing 155° at a range of 11000 yards. Enemy course was 270°. P 48's course was 030°. The submarine turned to 210° and closed at full speed for 10 minutes.
By 1441 A/12, it was clear that the range had opened. Only the tops of the masts could be seen and the attack was broken off.

In a signal timed 1034 A/12 the Capt. S.10 had stated that an aircraft had reported the following at 1015 A/12; Position of two medium vessels and five destroyers 38°00'N, 11°30'E. course 315°, speed 10 knots.

Lt. Faber estimated that this reported convoy could not reach the position of P 48 before 1700 hours even assuming that it might be going a little faster than reported. It's arrival at 1430 A/12 gave it a speed of nearly 20 knots or else the position as reported was greatly in error. The convoy passed to the south of Cani Rock.
At 1611 A/12, the Capt. S.10's signals timed 1445 A/12 and 1535 A/12 were received.

The signal timed 1445 A/12 contained the following; Air reconnaissance shows Bizerta partially blocked and it appears probable that shipping must anchor off the breakwater. Submerged attack by moonlight as early as possible appears hopeful, seaward patrols must be expected.
The signal 1535 A/12 contained the following; Cancel my 1445 A/12. The outer harbour can still be entered by merchant ships.
At 1632 A/12, HE was heard on bearing 202°, Four destroyers were sighted proceeding on course 040°.
Shortly afterwards it was seen this were four Soldati-class destroyer proceeding at 20 knots.
[The four destroyers of the convoy escort returning to Italy ???]
P 48 then closed the breakwater but no shipping was seen in Bizerta roads. (16)

13 Nov 1942 HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) attacked an enemy merchant vessel with three torpedoes about 10 nautical miles north-east of Bizerte, Tunisia in position 37°23'N, 10°00'E. No hits were obtained.

This was probably the Italian motor ship Città di Napoli (5418 GRT, built 1929) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Clio bound for Palermo after a transport mission to Tunisia. The attack was unobserved.
1025 A/13 - Sighted a merchant vessel of about 7000 tons escorted by a destroyer bearing 197°. Started attack.
1050 A/13 - In position 37°23'N, 10°00'E fired three torpedoes at the merchant vessel from a range of 5400 yards. No hits were obtained and no counter attack followed.
1200 A/13 - Reloaded.
1443 A/13 - Sighted a French trawler.
1605 A/13 - Sighted a German motor torpedo boat (E-boat) bearing 150° proceeding towards Bizerta.
This must have been one out of the following; S 57, S 35 and S 30 which had departed Tunis around 1415 A/13 and arrived at Bizerta around 1630 A/13. (17)
14 Nov 1942 HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) attacked an escorted merchant vessel north-east of Bizerta, Tunisia in position 37°20'N, 10°00'E with three torpedoes. No hits were obtained.
This was the Italian transport Sivigliano (1270 GRT, built 1921) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Sirtori on a transport mission from Palermo to Bizerta.

0027 A/14 - Received an enemy reported timed 2352 A/13 which contained the following; Position, course and speed of one merchant vessel and two destroyers at 2340 A/13, 37°48'N, 10°57'E. 220°, 10 knots.
[Citta di Tunisi and her escort ???]
0525 A/14 - Dived. Proceeded inshore.
0825 A/14 - sighted aircraft circling to the north-east.
0828 A/14 - Heard an explosion. Concluded that a convoy was approaching.
0838 A/14 - Sighted a merchant vessel of about 3000 tons escorted by a General class torpedo boat and three aircraft bearing 039°, range 11000 yards. Started attack.
0905 A/14 - The air escort left the convoy.
0930 A/14 - In position 37°20'N, 10°00'E fired three torpedoes at the merchant vessel. Enemy course was 230°, speed 9 knots. No hits were obtained.
0937 A/14 - 0952 A/14 - A motor torpedo boat, which had approached from the southward, dropped six single depth charges. No damage was done to P 48.
[Apparently not a German E-boat, perhaps Italian ?]

1030 A/14 - Quiet allround. Came to periscope depth. When at 35 feet HE was reported bearing 070°. Sighted a large transport proceeding on course 210° escorted by an unknown number of destroyers. This ship resembled the Italian Virgilio.
1850 A/14 - Surfaced.

1954 A/14 - Passed the signal timed 1831 A/14 which contained the following; All torpedoes expended [Remember that on departure from Gibraltar only six torpedoes had been on board due to the two Folbots embarked for the special operation], nothing hit. Am returning to Gibraltar through position 37°54'N, 07°59'E at 0600 A/15. No A/S patrol in Bizerta Roads but great aircraft activity including many transports to and from daily. Have informed HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN) [through S.S./T.] that my billet is vacant. [A weather report was also included.] (6)

15 Nov 1942 At 0205 A/15, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0031 A/15 which contained the following orders; Your 1831 A/14. You are to proceed to Malta through positions 38°00'N, 09°00'E and 38°20'N, 10°00'E, thence direct to position 'B' (37°48'N, 11°44'E) to arrive there at 0500 A/16 to make passage of Q.B.B. 255 (mined area) during daylight as laid down in the V.A. Malta's signal timed 1215 A/10(October). You should be at position 'F' (36°10'N, 14°00'E) by dawn/17 thence on the surface to make rendezvous with minesweepers at end of the searched channel at 1325 A/17. HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) will be passing westbound through Q.B.B. 255 during daylight/16 at 80 feet.

At 1630 A/15, HMS P 48 surfaced to pass her signal timed 1629 A/15 which contained the following; Your 0031 A/15. Have been delayed by bad weather. Intend making the passage of Q.B.B. 255 during daylight/17. [A weather report was also included in the signal]. My position 38°12'N, 09°36'E.
At 1655 A/15, the message was passed successfully.
At 1700 A/15, the first part of the Vice Admiral's signal timed 1538 A/15 was received.
At 2017 A/15, the second part of the Vice Admiral's signal timed 1538 A/15 was received.

This signal contains the following; 1) HMS Welshman (Capt. W.H.D. Friedberger, DSO, RN) expects to pass through position 38°05'N, 10°55'E at 0100 A/16 thence to Malta through Q.B.B. 255 keeping approximately to submarine route and proceeding at high speed. 2) HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) left Malta at 1200 A/15 by reverse of HMS Welshman's route arriving in the proximity of position 37°19'N, 12°49'E, about 0600 A/16. 3) HMS P 48 with no torpedoes passes through position 38°20'N, 10°00'E, at 1800 A/15th thence through Q.B.B. 255 by same route as HMS Welshman to Malta. (18)
18 Nov 1942 At 1615 A/18, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) ended her 4th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN), were swept in by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR) which had joined around 1400 A/18. (6)
26 Nov 1942 At 1300 A/26, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) departed Malta for her 5th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol north of Tunisia.
She and HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN), were escorted out until 1520 A/26-1530 A/26 by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN).
Both submarines were to proceed via the usual route through Q.B.B. 255 (mined area) and then through positions 38°14'N, 11°21'E and 38°05'N, 10°15'E to their patrol area's. (19)
26 Nov 1942 HMS P 48 during her 5th war patrol see the map below. [As no log is available no daily positions can be shown.]
27 Nov 1942 At 0540 A/27, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) dived in position 37°15'N, 13°00'E.
At 0851 A/27, while P 48 was deep, HE was heard bearing 110°. This sounded like turbine doing 280 revolutions.

At 0859 A/27, a noise was heard as of a torpedo being fired and running overhead. This was followed by five more at approximately one minute intervals. On each occasion, the submarine shook in an exactly similar manner as when firing torpedoes.
P 48 remained deep and did not investigate.
At 1830 A/27, HMS P 48 surfaced.

At 2015 A/27, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1647 A/27 was received in which she was ordered to be part of a patrol line which was established in direction 120° from position 040° - Cani Rock - 4 miles. HMS P 48 was ordered to take up position 'W' in the line at dawn on the 28th.
At 2246 A/27, a darkened vessel was seen bearing 280° at a range of 4 miles. This appeared to be a destroyer.
At 2252 A/27, HMS P 48 dived in position 37°46'N, 11°33'E [The patrol report gives position 38°46'N, 11°33'E but this must be a mistake] as the moon was behind the submarine. No HE was picked up.
At 2324 A/27, HMS P 48 surfaced. Nothing was in sighted and P 48 proceeded. (20)
28 Nov 1942 At 2100 A/28, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) arrived in the ordered patrol position.
At 2235 A/28, HE was picked up bearing 275°.

At 2245 A/28, the wake of a fast moving vessel was sighted bearing 330° at a range of 1 mile. There was a poor horizon in this direction but the enemy appeared to be a motor torpedo boat. The HE that was picked up was however from turbine engines doing 210 revolutions. Approximate course of the enemy was 040°. P 48 dived.
At 2310 A/28, the HE faded on bearing 063°.

At 2324 A/28, HMS P 48 surfaced and received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2200 A/28 which stated that HMS P 48 might have been sighted at 1535 A/28. At that time she had been patrolling at periscope depth. (20)

29 Nov 1942 At 1910 A/29, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1254 A/29 in which she was ordered to patrol in position 37°21'N, 10°40'E (north of Tunis Bay). Course was set to comply with these orders. (20)
1 Dec 1942 At 0935 A/1, in position 37°23'N, 10°39'E, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) sighted smoke bearing 065°.

At 0938 A/1, an aircraft, a Cant. Z.506B, was seen weaving ahead of the smoke. P 48 was in an ideal position to intercept the enemy if he would proceed to Bizerta either north or south of Cani Rocks but approximately 8 miles off track if he proceeded direct to Tunis. P 48 turned stern on to the bearing until the enemy's course could be estimated.
By 0957 A/1, it was noticed from the general behaviour of the air escort that the enemy was making for Tunis. Course was altered to 160° and speed was increased to full speed submerged.
At 1015 A/1, the smoke was still in sight but the aircraft was no longer seen.
At 1018 A/1, nothing was seen anymore.
At 1023 A/1, the smoke and the aircraft were seen again.
At 1100 A/1, the enemy had still not been sighted and not even smoke was now in sight so the 'attack' was broken off.
At 1123 A/1, smoke and masts were seen bearing 103° at a range of about 10 miles. Recommenced attack as the enemy appeared to have zigged towards. Closed at full speed.

By 1148 A/1, it was clear that the enemy was going to pass eastwards of Zembra Island so the attack was once more abandoned hoping that HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) would be able to cope with this enemy vessel. No other escort other than the aircraft was seen.
At 1230 A/1, the same vessel was seen again having passed east and south of Zembra Island.
[Possibly Valdirosa ???, see the page of P 45.]

At 2325 A/1, HE was picked up between bearings 020° and 080°. P 48 closed on the surface to investigate. These was a thick black cloud on the horizon from 010° to 100° and it was impossible to see anything against it. It was reported that the HE sounded 'massed, like a convoy'.
At 2347 A/1, the HE ceased on bearing 100°. (6)
2 Dec 1942 At 0045 A/2, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) observed gunfire to the northward.
At 0222 A/2, a darkened ship was sighted bearing 280° which looked like a destroyer proceeding on course 270°. P 48 went to full speed to investigate.
At 0225 A/2, the destroyer was observed to be closing on course 100°. He fired a salvo at this time, to the southward.
At 0236 A/2, HMS P 48 dived. HE doing 220 revolutions was picked up.
At 0248 A/2, the HE faded out on bearing 180°.

What P 48 had sighted was an enemy convoy being decimated by ' Force Q ', which was made up of the light cruisers HMS Aurora (Capt. W.G. Agnew, CB, RN, flying the flag of Rear-Admiral C.H.J. Harcourt, CBE, RN), HMS Argonaut (Capt. E.W.L. Longley-Cook, RN), HMS Sirius (Capt. P.W.B. Brooking, RN) and the destroyers HMS Quentin (Lt.Cdr. A.H.P. Noble, DSC, RN) and HMAS Quiberon (Cdr. H.W.S. Browning, OBE, RN).
At 0304 A/2, HMS P 48 surfaced. Nothing further was sighted during daylight.
At 1831 A/2, three dark objects were seen bearing 080° at a range of 4 miles. Course was set to investigate and attack of possible.
At 1840 A/2, it was seen that these three vessels were making much smoke.
At 1850 A/2, two of the ships had disappeared from sight owing to the large amount of smoke. It was difficult to see what the targets were and what they were doing.
At 1911 A/2, it appeared that the target had turned end on.
At 1935 A/2, the target appeared to be steering 180°. P 48 increased to full speed to gain bearing.
At 1950 A/2, the target faded to the southward to pass east of Zembra Island. Broke off the attack. The target could not be identified. (6)
3 Dec 1942 HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) attacked a transport ship from 'convoy B' north of the Gulf of Tunis in position 37°22'N, 10°37'E. No hits were obtained.

'Convoy B' was made up of the German transports Menes (5609 GRT, built 1926) and Lisboa (1999 GRT, built 1911), The Italian transports Arlesiana (5702 GRT, built 1915), Achille Lauro (5209 GRT, built 1921) and Campania (5247 GRT, built 1918). The were escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Sirio, Groppo, Orione, Animoso, Antonio Mosto and Uragano. The torpedo boat Groppo attacked the submarine with fifteen depth charges in a first run followed by a second one with the same number before rejoining the convoy. The submarine was claimed as 'probably damaged' but Supermarina rejected the claim.
0540 A/3 - Dived.
0740 A/3 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0451 A/3 in which P 48 was ordered to remain in her present ordered patrol position.
0852 A/3 - Sighted a sailing vessel bearing 063° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 238°. Did not attack to await bigger game.
0950 A/3 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0945 A/3 in which P 48 was ordered to proceed on course 305° - 5 miles.
1030 A/3 - Sighted a Cant. Z.506 aircraft and smoke bearing 005°

1039 A/3 - Sighted masts and funnels of four merchant vessels and two destroyers bearing 360°. Started attack. Ran in at full speed. The convoy zigged towards putting P 48 in a very good attack position just inside the port wing destroyer of the Navigatori-class.
1114 A/3 - In position fired 37°22'N, 10°37'E four torpedoes at the leading ship, a 6000 tons merchant vessel. The port wing destroyer was about 400 yards astern. No hits were obtained.
1122 A/3 - Nine depth charges were dropped fairly close but above. P 48 meanwhile withdrew on a course of 080°.

1155 A/3 - Returned to periscope depth. The convoy was sighted bearing 230° at a range of 5 nautical miles. The Navigatori-class destroyer was seen bearing 340°, range 1.5 nautical miles on a course of 080°. Went deep again.
1235 A/3 - Returned to periscope depth. The convoy was now out of sight.
1315 A/3 - Reloaded. Retired on course 052°.
1340 A/3 - Observed a large column of smoke bearing 230°.
1830 A/3 - Surfaced.

1900 A/3 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1041 A/3 which contained enemy reports by aircraft, 1115 A/3 which was an amplification signal of the 1041 A/3 signal and the signal timed 1609 A/3 in which submarines retiring to the north were ordered to attack any large northbound ship encountered.
1915 A/3 - Set course to withdraw to the southern Tyrrhenian Sea in compliance with the orders from the Capt. S.10 as given in his signal timed 0438 A/3.

2110 A/3 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1551 A/3 ordering to withdraw through Q.B.B. 255. Set course for position 'H' keeping 5 miles clear from HMS P 219 (Lt. N.L.A. Jewell, RN). Set course for position 'H' (37°26'N, 11°07'E).
2240 A/3 - HMS P 48 sighted another submarine bearing 190° at a range of 1.5 miles. This is thought to be HMS P 219 [which reported nothing at this time though.]
2305 A/3 - Arrived in position 'H' (37°26'N, 11°07'E).
2314 A/3 - Sighted a dark object bearing 125° which was thought to be HMS P 219 once more. (6)

3 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 2359 A/3, the Capt. S.10 sent the following signal to HMS P 217 (Lt. E.J.D. Turner, DSC, RN), HMS P 219 (Lt. N.L.A. Jewell, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN); P 217 and P 219 proceed to the Naples area with despatch by proceeding on surface by day at the Commanding Officers discretion to patrol within 8 miles of the following positions; P 217, 40°47'N, 13°48'E and P 219, 40°35'N, 14°08'E. HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN) is expected to join you shortly.

P 37, P 43 and P 48 proceed with despatch but submerged by day to form a patrol line (operation B.S.). 3 - 350° -Cape San Vito, with the first submarine 4 miles from Cape San Vito and the other submarine spaced 5 miles apart.
P 45 return to establish patrol approximately 40° from Plane Island lighthouse in position selected by you for interception of Bizerta traffic. (21)

3 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 0438 A/3, the Capt. S.10 sent the following signal to HMS P 219 (Lt. N.L.A. Jewell, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN); On completion of attacks [on an expected convoy] you are to withdraw either the the north-east to the Tyrrhenian Sea or via the route of HMS Trooper (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSO, DSC, RN) as in my 0005 A/1 to the south-east of Pantelleria.

[HMS Trooper's route had been through the following positions according the signal timed 0005 A/1 from the Capt. S.10; 'G' (38°00'N, 11°25'E), 'H' (37°26'N, 11°07'E), 'J' (37°04'N, 11°40'E) and 'K' (36°24'N, 11°49'E).]

In a signal timed 0451 A/3, these submarines were sent the following orders; Assume the following dispositions forthwith; P 219, 37°28'N, 11°00'E. P 37, 37°24'N, 10°51'E. P 45, 37°13'N, 10°25'E. P 48 and P 43, remain in your present ordered positions. Position, course and speed of the enemy convoy at 0205 A/3, 37°50'N, 11°38'E, 235°, 8 knots. Convoy of four merchant ships and five destroyers.

In a signal timed 0845 A/3, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 37, HMS P 43 and HMS P 48; Move with utmost despatch from ordered position as follows; P 37, 10 miles 305°. P 48, 5 miles 305°. P 43, 5 miles 340°. (21)
4 Dec 1942 At 0200 A/4, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) sighted Pantelleria bearing 130°.

At 0205 A/4, HMS 48 received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2359 A/3. [For the text of this signal see one of the 'events' for 3 December 1942.] Altered course to comply with the orders contained in this signal.
At 1150 A/4, smoke and an aircraft (Cant. Z.506B) and smoke was sighted bearing 230°. This appeared to be drawing ahead.
At 1216 A/4, speed was increased to full submerged speed to close.
At 1235 A/4, the smoke was bearing 296°. It seemed that the enemy was on approximate course 065. As there seemed little hope to catch the northbound enemy the chase was abandoned.
At 1300 A/4, Marettimo was sighted bearing 062°.
At 1530 A/4, in position 37°45'N, 11°22'E an abandoned lifeboat was passed.
At 1915 A/4, while now on the surface, a vessel was seen bearing 075°, which was sweeping the horizon with a very powerful searchlight.

At 1918 A/4, an aircraft passed overhead. The searchlight was seen sweeping towards so P 48 dived surfacing again at 1931 A/4 when the vessel was seen still sweeping with the searchlight about 5 miles away. Lt. Faber proceeded to the westward to get clear.
At 1943 A/4, the vessel finally turned off the searchlight and course was altered to 360°.
At 2036 A/4, when well clear, course was altered to make for the ordered position. (6)

5 Dec 1942 HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) attacked an escorted merchant vessel with four torpedoes about 25 nautical miles north of Cape San Vito, Sicily in position 38°34'N, 12°40'E. No hits were obtained. P 48 was depth charged following this attack.

These were the German transport Süllberg (1661 GRT, built 1912) and the German tanker Jaedjoer (308 GRT, built 1937) on passage from Naples to Trapani and eventually to Tunisia. They had been met by the Italian torpedo boat Perseo. Two torpedo tracks were sighted from Süllberg and Perseo carried out a counter attack, dropping a large number of depth charges, and claiming the submarine sunk. For Süllberg it was only a reprieve as she was sunk four days later by the submarine HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN).
0545 A/5 - Dived.
0715 A/5 - Sighted a destroyer bearing 230° proceeding on a course of 200°.
1015 A/5 - The destroyer passed 5000 yards ahead. It subsequently identified as a Spica-class torpedo boat.
1155 A/5 - The enemy torpedo boat made off on bearing 075° having patrolled in the area all forenoon.
1225 A/5 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1058 A/5. [For the text of this signal see the other 'event' for 5 December 1942.] Altered course to comply with the orders.
1340 A/5 - Sighted smoke bearing 020°.
1432 A/5 - Sighted masts and funnel of one merchant vessel and masts of one destroyer and another small craft bearing 022°. Started attack.
1500 A/5 - The destroyer was seen to be a Spica-class torpedo boat.
1526 A/5 - In position 38°34'N, 12°40'E fired four torpedoes at the merchant vessel which was of about 4000 tons. Range was 3000 yards. Enemy course 225°, speed 8 knots. No hits were obtained.
1550 A/5 - Depth charging started. A total of 64 depth charges were counted until 1706 hours.
1845 A/5 - Surfaced and proceeded to the north-west.
2100 A/5 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1841 A/5 which stated that the decyphering table was compromised. Course was set to proceed to position 'G' (38°00'N, 11°25'E).
2230 A/5 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1909 A/5 in which P 48 was ordered to take up a new patrol position. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.] (6)

5 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1058 A/5, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 217 (Lt. E.J.D. Turner, DSC, RN), HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN); Withdraw 50 miles from the coast, orders will follow. (21)

7 Dec 1942 At 0045 A/7, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) left patrol to return to Malta through Q.B.B. 255 (mined area), 3 miles to the west of the route as had been ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0005 A/1. This route was as follows; Position 'G' (38°00'N, 11°25'E), Position 'H' (37°26'N, 11°07'E), Position 'J' (37°04'N, 11°40'E) and Position 'K' (36°24'N, 11°49'E).
At 0540 A/1 - P 48 dived in position 'J'. (6)
8 Dec 1942 At 0540 A/8, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) cleared her signal timed 0458 A/8 in which she reported her E.T.A. at Malta.

At 0755 A/8, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0620 A/8 was received which gave the time of the rendezvous with the minesweeper. At 1430 A/8, HMS P 48 ended her 5th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was not escorted in.
It seems likely that following the depth charging on this patrol P 48 was docked before proceeding on her next patrol. (6)

23 Dec 1942 HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) departed Malta for her 6th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Tunis, within 15 miles of position 37°06'N, 10°35'E. (22)

25 Dec 1942 At 1125 A/25, in 37°15’N, 10°39’E, the Italian transports XXI Aprile (4787 GRT, built 1919) and Carlo Zeno (1446 GRT, built 1906) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Ardito and Ardente were proceeding from Palermo to Tunis when an escorting aircraft fired bursts of machine gun fire in the sea to signal a submarine. Almost at the same moment the torpedo boat Ardente obtained a contact carried out several attack runs, dropping a total of 48 depth charges. An oil patch of about 100-150 metres in diameter was observed in 37°17'N, 10°32'E and the submarine was claimed sunk. It is quite likely that the submarine was HMS P 48 and that she was sunk during this encounter. There were no survivors.
Could HMS P 48 have survived this attack and sank later? This is not impossible but until her wreck is found we will never know for sure.

29 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 0025 A/29, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) which contained the following; My 1715 A/28. Amend your position to read 37°00'N, 10°45'E by 0959 A/29.
[In this signal the Capt. S.10 referred to his signal timed 1715 A/28. We have been unable to find the text of this signal.]
In a signal timed 1101 A/29 the Capt. S.10 cancelled his signal timed 0025 A/29. HMS P 48 was given freedom of action in her area. (21)
30 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1531 A/30, HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN) was given the following orders; Establish patrol by 0601 A/31 within 10 miles of position 37°31'N, 10°59'E. (21)

31 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1736 A/31, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN); Leave patrol at 0301 A/1 and proceed to Algiers through the following positions; 'A' = 37°52'N, 11°12'E. 'B' = 38°20'N, 10°52'E. 'C' = 38°20'N, 08°00'E thence as ordered by Capt. S.8. During the night of 1 / 2 December you are to report your E.T.A. when passing 08°00'E to the Capt. S.8.
In a signal timed 1217 A/4 the Capt. S.10 reported that the came no answer to the above signal and that P 48 was presumed to have been lost. (21)
ADM numbers indicate documents at the British National Archives at Kew, London.
